title: [WIP] Unit-Level Estimation and MRP
text:
Unit-level small area estimation (SAE) models the survey microdata directly. Instead of first calculating one direct estimate per area, the model uses individual or household records, predicts outcomes for the target population, and then aggregates those predictions to the small-area geography.
For example, suppose the estimand is:
The survey records might contain each respondent’s bus dissatisfaction status, age group, disability status, employment status, car access, and location. A unit-level model can use those respondent-level predictors directly.

title: [WIP] Unit-Level Estimation and MRP
text:
1 Unit-Level vs Area-Level SAE
Area-level and unit-level SAE are different ways to organise the data before modelling.









Approach
Model input
What gets modelled
How the area estimate is produced




Area-level SAE
One direct estimate per area
Area-level estimates
Direct estimates are smoothed using area covariates


Unit-level SAE
Survey records
Individual or household outcomes
Predicted unit outcomes are aggregated to areas



In an area-level model, the bus dissatisfaction survey data would first be collapsed into one direct estimate for each MSOA. The model would then use MSOA-level covariates, such as car ownership, population density, deprivation, or public transport access.
In a unit-level model, the survey stays at respondent level. The model can learn, for example, whether dissatisfaction differs by age group, disability status, employment status, or car access. Those predictions are then combined with population data to estimate each MSOA.
The basic unit-level structure is:
\[
y_{di} =
x_{di}^{\top}\beta + u_d + e_{di},
\]
where \(y_{di}\) is the outcome for person \(i\) in area \(d\), \(x_{di}\) contains individual or household predictors, and \(u_d\) is an area effect.
After fitting the model, the small-area estimate is produced by averaging predicted outcomes over the target population:
\[
\theta_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in U_d} \hat{y}_{di}.
\]
This is why unit-level SAE is often described as a bottom-up approach: model people or households first, then aggregate to places.

title: [WIP] Unit-Level Estimation and MRP
section: 2 Two Ways to Aggregate Unit-Level Predictions
text:
2 Two Ways to Aggregate Unit-Level Predictions
Once a unit-level model has been fitted to the survey records, the next question is how to apply the model to the target population. There are two common routes:








Route
Population data needed
Basic idea




Full population prediction
Population microdata or a synthetic population
Predict for every population unit, then average within each area


MRP
Counts for population cells
Predict for each cell, then weight by cell counts



The model itself can still include individual predictors, household predictors, area predictors, and area random effects. The distinction here is not the model structure; it is the format of the population data used to turn predictions into area estimates.

title: [WIP] Unit-Level Estimation and MRP
text:
3 Full Population Prediction
The most direct route is to predict for every person or household in the target population.
For the bus dissatisfaction example, this would require a population or synthetic population file where each row is a person or household, with the same variables used in the survey model: age group, disability status, employment status, car access, and MSOA.
The workflow is:

Fit the model using survey respondents.
Predict bus dissatisfaction for every population record.
Average the predictions within each MSOA.

This approach is intuitive, but it requires detailed population microdata. In many applied settings, those data are unavailable, restricted, or only available through a synthetic population.

title: [WIP] Unit-Level Estimation and MRP
text:
4 MRP
MRP stands for Multilevel Regression and Poststratification. It uses population cell counts instead of full population microdata.
The regression part models the survey outcome using individual predictors and, often, area effects. The poststratification part applies those predictions to the known population composition of each area.
For example, a population cell might be:

Recent adult bus riders aged 25-34, employed, without car access, living in MSOA W02000001

The model predicts the dissatisfaction rate for that cell. The population table gives the number of people in that cell. The MSOA estimate is then a weighted average across all cells:
\[
\theta_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{j \in J_d} N_{dj}\hat{y}_{dj},
\]
where \(j\) indexes population cells, \(N_{dj}\) is the number of people in cell \(j\) in area \(d\), and \(\hat{y}_{dj}\) is the model prediction for that cell.
MRP is useful when individual-level predictors matter, but the population data are available as cross-tabulated counts rather than a row-by-row population file. It is best understood as a practical implementation of unit-level SAE under a different population-data format.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text: SAE Toolkit Lite is meant for quick adaptation of the materials to another survey, geography, or indicator. It assumes an area-level SAE workflow and a Bayesian modelling approach.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
1 Minimum inputs and recommended schema
A SAE workflow usually needs:

a survey indicator file, with one row per sampled unit used in the estimate
a domain-size file, with one row per estimation area
a covariate file, when fitting an area-level model
boundary geometry to map results or derive spatial structure, if relevant
(optional) population unit file when fitting unit-level SAE


1.1 Survey indicator file:
A survey indicator file links each sampled unit to the chosen outcome, estimation area, and survey weight. Survey documentation should be kept alongside the file so that routing, missingness, design variables, and weight choice can be checked.



Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent, household, or sampled unit ID.


domain_id
Estimation area code.


indicator
Numeric outcome, or binary 0/1.


weight
Survey weight selected for the estimand.


stratum
(optional) survey stratum.





1.2 Domain-size file
If available, a domain-size file gives the denominator for each estimation area. The denominator must match the estimand’s target population rather than the full resident population by default.



Column
Meaning




domain_id
Estimation area code.


domain_size
Denominator matching the target population.





1.3 Geometry file
Boundary geometry is useful for visualising estimates and for creating adjacency matrix for spatial models.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Estimation area code, matching the domain-size file.


domain_name
Human-readable area name.


geometry
Polygon or multipolygon geometry for the estimation area.





1.4 Covariate file for area-level estimation
Covariates are area-level predictors available for every estimation area. They should be measured on the same geography as the direct estimates, or aggregated to that geography before modelling.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Estimation area code.


covariate_*
Area-level predictor available for every estimation area.





1.5 Files for unit-level estimation
Unit-level SAE needs two harmonised files:

The survey_unit_data file besides indicator also needs covariates used to fit the model








Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent, household, or sampled unit ID.


domain_id
Estimation area code.


indicator
Outcome to model.


weight
Survey weight selected for the estimand.


stratum
(optional) survey stratum.


covariate_*
Individual or household covariate.




The population_unit_data file provides the prediction population (real or synthetic microdata), with one row per population unit, household, or population cell. The covariates must match those in survey_unit_data.








Column
Meaning




unit_id
Population person, household, or synthetic unit ID.


domain_id
Estimation area code used to aggregate predictions.


covariate_*
Matching individual or household covariate also used in survey_unit_data.


pop_weight
Population count represented by the row (optional, in case population cells are used)





1.6 Recommended output schema
For diagnostics, each estimation script should end with an area-level results table using the same column names. This makes it easier to combine direct, area-level, spatial, and unit-level outputs.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Estimation area code.


method
Estimation method label.


estimate
Point estimate for the estimation area.


se
Standard error or posterior standard deviation, where available.


lower
Lower confidence or credible interval bound.


upper
Upper confidence or credible interval bound.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
2 Downloadable scripts
Reusable scripts can be adapted once the input files follow the schema above.



Script
Purpose




direct_estimates.R
Calculate direct estimates by estimation area, sampling variances, and confidence intervals.


area_level_sae.R
Fit Bayesian area-level model with covariates and spatial structure derived from boundary geometry, if relevant.


unit_level_sae.R
Fit Bayesian unit-level model using survey microdata and population or synthetic-population covariates.


diagnostics.R
Create comparison tables and plots for checking estimates, uncertainty, and model outputs.

title: Overview
text:
Raw survey microdata is transformed into analysis-ready inputs by defining the estimand, coding the indicator from the survey variables, aligning it with the estimation geography, and choosing a compatible survey weight.
The data preparation pipeline can be summarised as follows:

title: Overview
text:
1 Define the Estimands
The estimand determines how survey microdata inputs are prepared and how to choose the most appropriate estimation approach downstream.
E.g.,

Share of adults who use the train weekly by MSOA
Mean leisure travel distance among retirees by Ward

The composition of a well-formed estimand is:

Estimate the measure of what outcome, among which population, across which geography.

WE will take a closer look at each of these components and the implications on the methodology.

1.1 Estimation geography
In SAE, the groups or areas being estimated are often called domains. In the spatial context such as urban and transport research, they are better understood as estimation areas instead. An estimation area is one unit in the chosen estimation geography. In the UK, they can be one Local Authority, Ward, LSOA, or MSOA.



Census Geography hierarchy going from Country to LA to MSOA to LSOA to OA (Source)


The chosen estimation geography must match the level of spatial detail available in the survey data. For example, if respondents are identified only by Local Authority, then Local Authority is the finest geography that can be used for small-area estimation. SAE methods are not intended to “upsample” survey data to a more granular spatial resolution.


1.2 Measure & Outcome
Based on the estimands, identify or create the survey indicator that represents the outcome and how they should be aggregated for each estimation area, by using resources such as the survey data user guide, survey questionnaire, response codes, and routing rules to guide indicator construction. Some indicators are direct recodes of one survey question; others need to combine several variables. The same raw survey variable can support different estimands.
The most common types of outcome indicator are:








Indicator type
What to define
Example




Binary
What is 1, 0, missing, and out of scope. Also suitable for categorical variables
Estimand: Share of adults who use the train weeklyIndicator: Uses train weekly: yes/no


Continuous
The numeric value and unit
Estimand: Mean leisure travel distance among retirees Indicator: Leisure travel distance in miles



The most common choices of measure, i.e., how the outcome indicators are aggregated, are:

share (e.g., % of EV among car owners)
mean (e.g., average trip duration)
rate in population (e.g., accidents per 1,000)
count/total (uncommon)

title: Overview
text:
2 Weights and domain sizes
Survey weights connect the sample back to the population to ensure the survey sample can best represent real-world proportions. The survey user guide is the go-to resource to understand which weight variables are available, what they adjust for, and most importantly, which survey modules/questions they apply to. In general, weight variables provided by surveys have already been derived from one or more adjustments, such as:

Selection adjustment: accounts for unequal probabilities of selection.
Non-response adjustment: compensates for differences between respondents and non-respondents.
Calibration adjustment: aligns the weighted sample with known population totals, such as age, sex, region, Local Authority, tenure, or household structure.
Subsample adjustment: accounts for cases where only part of the sample was asked a module or completed a diary.

For SAE, there is no one-size-fits-all approach to survey weight selection for the estimand. The weight used must match the estimand unit, target population, and survey design. Moreover, it is important to distinguish the scale of the weight variable:

Analysis weights \(w^{A}\) make means or proportions representative of the target population, and they generally sum to the sample size. These weights are most common for estimating share or mean.

E.g., if a sampling unit has an analysis weight of 0.5, it is over-represented in the sample relative to the population, so its contribution is ‘down-weighted’.

Grossing weights \(w^{G}\) are essentially upscaled-\(w^{A}\) so that they sum to a known population total within the relevant estimation areas. They are usually required if the estimand measure is count, or rate.

E.g., if a sampling unit has a grossing weight of 50, it means this one sampled row represents about 50 people in the population.


In SAE, domain size \(N_d\) defines the size of the target population in each estimation area, or domain. For example, if the estimation geography is Local Authority and the target population is adults aged 16+, then \(N_d\) is the number of adults aged 16+ in each Local Authority. Domain sizes are usually obtained from Census data, population estimates, administrative data, or other regional statistics. The correct domain size must match both the target population and the estimation geography used in the estimand.
They can be used to scale analysis weights into grossing weights in order to support estimands that depend on the actual number of people, households, or units in an area. For example:

If estimating the number of households who struggle with transport costs by Ward, \(N_d\) would be the total household count in each ward. It is used to scale sample-based weights and estimates into a population-level ones.
If estimating a claimant rate per 1,000 working-age residents by MSOA, \(N_d\) would be the working-age population in each MSOA, used as the denominator.

For share or mean estimands, SAE is possible even when domain sizes are unknown, however with certain nuances in interpretation.






Tip



If analysis weights are provided and the relevant domain sizes are known, they can be rescaled into grossing weights for each survey respondent:
\[
w^G_{di} = w^A_{di} \times \frac{N_d}{\sum_{i \in d} w^A_{di}}
\]
where \(w^A_{di}\) is the analysis weight for sampled unit \(i\) in domain \(d\), \(w^G_{di}\) is the corresponding grossing weight, and \(N_d\) is that domain’s size.

title: Overview
text:
3 Preparation Summary
Putting everything together, each of the estimands of interest should have a short record as follows that will guide how we prepare input datasets for the SAE workflow. For example, a compact record could be written as:

Estimand
Indicator
Variable coding rule
Measure
Estimation geography
Weight variable




1
Share of adults who use the train weekly across MSOAs
is_freq_train_user
Binary: 1 if TrainUseFreq = 1; 0 if adult does not use the train weekly; null for invalid or refused responses
Share
MSOA
SampleAdultWeight


2
Mean leisure travel distance among retirees across Wards
leisure_travel_distance
Continuous: numeric miles from TravelWorkDistance; only retirees with valid leisure travel distance are in scope
Mean
Ward

title: [WIP] An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
[draft] Beginner-friendly Small Area Estimation (SAE) for researchers estimating survey-derived metrics across a whole country or region at fine geographic resolution.
The site has two tracks: a theory track that explains the SAE workflow, and a practical tutorial track that applies the workflow to a dataset. It uses the National Survey for Wales as the first worked survey.
The main practical uses R, Quarto, and SAE packages such as survey, sae, and SUMMER. Make sure you have latest R version installed.

title: [WIP] An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
1 Why Small Area Estimation?
[draft] Survey are often not precise enough for fine geographies such as Local Authorities, MSOAs, or LSOAs. Some areas have very few/no respondents, unequal weights Taking weighted meands can be unstable. Also out of domain sample
SAE is useful when direct estimates are too noisy or incomplete. Area-level SAE use direct estimates and their sampling variances, borrow strength across areas using covariates and random effects.
SAE can be further improved with bayesian statistics (to smooth out area) and add spatial structure (spatial autocorrelation)
SAE combines survey data, population estimates, auxiliary data, modelling to estimate the unsurveyed in an informed way.

title: Area-Level Estimation (Fay-Herriot)
text: The Fay-Herriot (FH) model for area-level estimation improves direct estimates that are too noisy, unstable, or unavailable for some areas by borrowing strength from area-level covariates and the spatial structure.

title: Area-Level Estimation (Fay-Herriot)
text:
1 Understanding the basic Fay-Herriot model
The Fay-Herriot model is an area-level small area estimation model. It combines two sources of information for each estimation area:

the direct survey estimate for the estimation area, and

the model-based prediction from covariates.

Their relationship can be written as:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d = x_d^\top \beta + u_d + e_d
\]
where:

\(\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d\) is the direct survey estimate for area \(d\);
\(x_d^\top \beta\) is the part explained by covariates (i.e., model prediction);
\(u_d\) is the area-specific unexplained effect;
\(e_d\) is the survey sampling error.

The Fay-Herriot estimate is then a weighted average between the direct estimate and the model-based prediction:
\[
\tilde{\theta}^{FH}_d =
\gamma_d \hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d
+
(1-\gamma_d)x_d^\top\hat{\beta}
\]
The shrinkage weight \(\gamma_d =\frac{\sigma_u^2}{\sigma_u^2 + \psi_d}\) controls how much the final estimate relies on the direct survey estimate versus the model-based prediction, using sampling variance \(\psi_d\) and between-area unexplained variance \(\sigma_u^2\) (i.e., variance of \(u_d\)).

If \(\psi_d\) is small (the direct estimate is precise), \(\gamma_d\) is closer to 1 and the FH estimate stays close to the direct estimate;
If \(\psi_d\) is large (the direct estimate is noisy), \(\gamma_d\) is closer to 0 and the FH estimate is pulled more strongly toward the model-based prediction;
If \(\sigma_u^2\) is large (strong unexplained differences among areas), the FH estimate gives more weight to the direct estimate.
If \(\sigma_u^2\) is small (weak unexplained differences among areas), the FH estimate is pulled more strongly toward the model-based prediction.

Essentially, the Fay-Herriot model evaluates how reliable the direct estimates are. Reliable direct estimates are mostly preserved, while noisy direct estimates borrow more information from the model.

title: Area-Level Estimation (Fay-Herriot)
text:
2 Select covariates
SAE improves precision of the estimates by “borrowing strength” from auxiliary variables (i.e., covariates). This is critical for estimation areas with sparse or no survey data. Covariates can often be found via census data and other population-level aggregate statistics. Choose covariates with emphasis on coverage and comparability across estimation areas.
The key attributes of good covariates are:

Coverage: each covariate should be available for all estimation areas.
Alignment: match geography, definition, and reference period to the survey indicator.
Relevance: keep variables with a clear substantive link to the outcome and avoid including many weak predictors.
Predictive: choose covariates that improve area-level prediction and are easy to explain.

title: Area-Level Estimation (Fay-Herriot)
text:
3 Spatial structure
The basic Fay-Herriot model usually assumes that the unexplained area effects \(u_d\) are independent after accounting for covariates. In urban research, this assumption is often unrealistic. Nearby areas may share infrastructure, socio-demographic profiles, transport conditions, etc. As a result, neighbouring areas may still have similar unexplained effects even after covariates have been included.
A spatial Fay-Herriot model relaxes this independence assumption by allowing the unexplained area effects to be spatially related. One way to express this is:
\[
u = \rho W u + \eta
\]
where:

\(u\) is the vector of unexplained area effects;
\(W\) is a spatial weights matrix describing which areas are neighbours (based on adjacency, distance, etc.);
\(\rho\) controls the strength of spatial dependence;
\(\eta\) is the remaining non-spatial random variation.

The weight matrix \(W\) defines which areas are treated as neighbours. The spatial Fay-Herriot model then estimates whether the unexplained effects in neighbouring areas tend to be similar.

If neighbouring areas have similar unexplained effects, the model estimates stronger spatial dependence. For noisy areas, the final estimate is shifted toward values suggested by neighbouring areas and the model prediction. This is also called spatial smoothing.
If neighbouring areas do not have similar unexplained effects, the spatial component has little influence. The model behaves more like a non-spatial Fay-Herriot model.

There are several ways to define the spatial weights matrix \(W\). The most common approach is contiguity-based adjacency, where two areas are neighbours if their boundaries touch.



Example of spatial weight matrix by adjacency








Warning



The spatial weights matrix is another modelling assumption. It should be checked carefully, especially for islands, coastal areas, or fragmented geographies, because isolated areas or unusual neighbour links can strongly affect spatial smoothing.

title: Area-Level Estimation (Fay-Herriot)
section: 4 Bayesian vs. Frequentist approach in SAE
text:
4 Bayesian vs. Frequentist approach in SAE
In small-area estimation, there are two statistical approaches to treating unknown quantities such as \(\beta\), \(\sigma_u^2\), and \(\gamma_d\), specifically how they are estimated and how uncertainty is interpreted.

In the frequentist approach, unknown quantities such as \(\beta\), \(\sigma_u^2\), and \(u_d\) are estimated as fixed unknowns. Typical outputs are one point estimate per area, uncertainty measures such as RMSE, and confidence intervals.
In the Bayesian approach, these unknown quantities are instead treated as uncertain and assigned prior distributions. Typical outputs are posterior means, credible intervals, and probability summaries.




The difference in uncertainty treatment between Bayesian and Frequentist approaches


In practice, the Bayesian approach is preferred since reporting uncertainty as probability distributions facilitates communication in policy settings and is more useful for decision-making.

title: Area-Level Estimation (Fay-Herriot)
text:
5 Practical tutorial
In this workbook, we fit Bayesian area-level FH models for the mean commute distance among commuters by MSOA estimand using the indicator commute_distance.
We first recreate the direct estimates and their sampling variances, then pass those area-level inputs into SUMMER::smoothArea() with MSOA-level covariates.


Code
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(survey)
library(SUMMER)
library(spdep)

# Load analysis-ready data into a temp folder
zip_tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-clean-data.zip"
download.file(url, zip_tmp, mode = "wb")
unzip(zip_tmp, exdir = tempdir())
base_path <- file.path(tempdir())

# Load Census files into a temp folder
census_zip_tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
census_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-census-data.zip"
download.file(census_url, census_zip_tmp, mode = "wb")
unzip(census_zip_tmp, exdir = tempdir())
census_path <- file.path(tempdir())

# Load shapefiles for plotting
boundary_zip <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
boundary_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-boundaries.zip"
download.file(boundary_url, boundary_zip, mode = "wb")
unzip(boundary_zip, exdir = tempdir())

msoa_boundaries <- st_read(
  file.path(tempdir(), "boundaries", "boundaries-msoa.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
) |>
  rename(domain_id = MSOA21CD) |>
  filter(str_starts(domain_id, "W"))

msoa_domains <- msoa_boundaries |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(domain_id) |>
  arrange(domain_id)



5.1 Load Indicator
Load the commute_distance indicator file. This indicator is continuous and is defined among respondents with a valid commute distance.


Code
survey_indicator_commute <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_commute_distance.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(
    !is.na(indicator),
    !is.na(weight),
    !is.na(domain_id),
    !is.na(strata)
  )




5.2 Select and aggregate covariates
For commute_distance, useful area-level covariates should capture urban-rural structure, commuting mode, car access, housing form, and the local employment mix. The Census 2021 gives us several candidate covariates at MSOA level.








Covariate
Census table
Why it may explain commute distance




cov_pop_density
TS006
Dense urban areas tend to have shorter trips and more nearby jobs/services.


cov_share_work_home
TS061
Areas with more home-working may have a different commuter composition.


cov_share_hh_withcar
TS045
Household car access indicates ability to travel longer distances.


cov_share_detached
TS044
Detached housing is a proxy for lower-density suburban/rural settlement.


cov_share_higher_managerial
TS062
Occupational structure may reflect access to different labour markets.









Note



We do not use Census commute-distance table TS058 as a covariate because it is reserved later as an external validation benchmark for this tutorial.




Code
# Read a Census 2021 aggregate table and keep Welsh areas only
read_census <- function(topic_code) {
  file_name <- paste0("census2021-", topic_code, "-msoa.csv")
  read_csv(file.path(census_path, file_name), show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
    rename(domain_id = `geography code`)
}

# Population density (TS006)
msoa_density <- read_census("ts006") |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    cov_pop_density = `Population Density: Persons per square kilometre; measures: Value`
  )

# Travel to work (TS061)
msoa_travel <- read_census("ts061") |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    commute_total = `Method of travel to workplace: Total: All usual residents aged 16 years and over in employment the week before the census`,
    cov_share_work_home = if_else(
      commute_total > 0,
      `Method of travel to workplace: Work mainly at or from home` / commute_total,
      NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  select(-commute_total)

# Car availability (TS045)
msoa_cars <- read_census("ts045") |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    household_total = `Number of cars or vans: Total: All households`,
    cov_share_hh_withcar = if_else(
      household_total > 0,
      (household_total - `Number of cars or vans: No cars or vans in household`) / household_total,
      NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  select(-household_total)

# Housing type (TS044)
msoa_housing <- read_census("ts044") |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    household_total = `Accommodation type: Total: All households`,
    cov_share_detached = if_else(
      household_total > 0,
      `Accommodation type: Detached` / household_total,
      NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  select(-household_total)

# Occupation structure (TS062)
msoa_nssec <- read_census("ts062") |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    nssec_total = `National Statistics Socio-economic Classification (NS-SEC): Total: All usual residents aged 16 years and over`,
    cov_share_higher_managerial = if_else(
      nssec_total > 0,
      `National Statistics Socio-economic Classification (NS-SEC): L1, L2 and L3 Higher managerial, administrative and professional occupations` / nssec_total,
      NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  select(-nssec_total)

covariates_commute_summer <- msoa_density |>
  left_join(msoa_travel, by = "domain_id") |>
  left_join(msoa_cars, by = "domain_id") |>
  left_join(msoa_housing, by = "domain_id") |>
  left_join(msoa_nssec, by = "domain_id")


Finally, we rescale the covariates to prepare them as inputs for the FH model.


Code
covariates_commute_summer <- covariates_commute_summer |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    cov_pop_density = as.numeric(scale(cov_pop_density)),
    cov_share_work_home = as.numeric(scale(cov_share_work_home)),
    cov_share_hh_withcar = as.numeric(scale(cov_share_hh_withcar)),
    cov_share_detached = as.numeric(scale(cov_share_detached)),
    cov_share_higher_managerial = as.numeric(scale(cov_share_higher_managerial))
  )




5.3 Direct Estimates
The direct estimate is recreated here so the Bayesian FH model receives the direct estimate and its sampling variance in the format expected by smoothArea().


Code
design_commute <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~1,
  strata = ~strata,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = survey_indicator_commute
)

commute_mean <- survey::svyby(
  formula = ~indicator,
  by = ~domain_id,
  design = design_commute,
  FUN = survey::svymean,
  vartype = "se",
  na.rm = TRUE
) |>
  tibble::as_tibble()

direct_est_commute <- commute_mean |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    direct = indicator,
    variance = se^2,
    direct_lower = direct - 1.96 * sqrt(variance),
    direct_upper = direct + 1.96 * sqrt(variance),
    direct_ci_width = direct_upper - direct_lower
  )

5.4 Fit Fay-Herriot Model
With all the components ready, we can now proceed to fitting a Fay-Herriot model on our estimates. For smoothArea(), we need to standardise the direct estimates. After fitting, the FH estimates are transformed back to the original reporting scale, and we can also extract the posterior variance, and 95% credible interval for each MSOA.






Note



For smoothArea(), the direct-estimate input must have the domain ID in the first column, the direct estimate in the second column, and the sampling variance in the third column.




Code
direct_center <- mean(direct_est_commute$direct, na.rm = TRUE)
direct_scale <- sd(direct_est_commute$direct, na.rm = TRUE)

direct_est_summer <- direct_est_commute |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    direct = (direct - direct_center) / direct_scale,
    variance = variance / direct_scale^2
  )

fh_formula <- direct ~ cov_pop_density +
  cov_share_work_home +
  cov_share_hh_withcar +
  cov_share_detached +
  cov_share_higher_managerial

fh_commute_fit <- SUMMER::smoothArea(
  formula = fh_formula,
  domain = ~domain_id,
  direct.est = direct_est_summer,
  X.domain = covariates_commute_summer,
  adj.mat = NULL,
  transform = "identity",
  level = 0.95
)


There are domains in X.domain not in design/direct estimates. 
Generating estimates for all domains in X.domain.


Code
fh_est_commute <- fh_commute_fit$iid.model.est |>
  tibble::as_tibble() |>
  transmute(
    domain_id = domain,
    fh_mean = (mean * direct_scale) + direct_center,
    fh_lower = (lower * direct_scale) + direct_center,
    fh_upper = (upper * direct_scale) + direct_center,
    fh_ci_width = fh_upper - fh_lower
  )

Code

The reason for this drastic shrinkage is easier to see when the estimates are ranked by the direct estimate and shown with their original uncertainty intervals. Areas with wide direct-estimate confidence intervals contribute less direct survey evidence, so their FH estimates are pulled more strongly toward the model prediction.

ranked_commute <- commute_comparison |>
  filter(has_direct) |>
  arrange(direct, domain_id) |>
  mutate(direct_estimate_rank = row_number())

  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = direct_lower, ymax = direct_upper), fill = "blue", alpha = 0.16) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = fh_lower, ymax = fh_upper), fill = "#54A24B", alpha = 0.16) +
  geom_line(aes(y = direct, colour = "Direct"), linewidth = 0.45, na.rm = TRUE) +
  geom_line(aes(y = fh_mean, colour = "Fay-Herriot"), linewidth = 0.45) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Direct" = "blue",
    "Fay-Herriot" = "#54A24B"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Direct vs. Fay-Herriot estimates and CIs\nMean Commute Distance",
    x = "MSOAs (ranked by direct estimate)",
    y = "Mean commute distance (miles)",
    colour = NULL

5.5 Fit Spatial Fay-Herriot Model
The non-spatial FH model borrows strength from covariates and the overall distribution of MSOAs, but it does not use the fact that MSOAs are geographically connected. For transport outcomes, this independence assumption may be unrealistic. We therefore fit a spatial FH model as a sensitivity extension. It asks whether allowing neighbouring MSOAs to have similar unexplained area effects changes the estimates or their uncertainty.
First, we create a spatial weights matrix using queen contiguity, where two MSOAs are neighbours if they share either a boundary segment or a point.


Code
msoa_boundaries_ordered <- msoa_boundaries |>
  arrange(domain_id)

msoa_neighbours <- spdep::poly2nb(
  msoa_boundaries_ordered,
  queen = TRUE,
  row.names = msoa_boundaries_ordered$domain_id
)

adjacency_matrix <- spdep::nb2mat(
  msoa_neighbours,
  style = "B",
  zero.policy = TRUE
)

rownames(adjacency_matrix) <- msoa_boundaries_ordered$domain_id
colnames(adjacency_matrix) <- msoa_boundaries_ordered$domain_id


Next, we refit the FH model, now specifying adj.mat in the smoothArea() function call.


Code
spatial_fh_commute_fit <- SUMMER::smoothArea(
  formula = fh_formula,
  domain = ~domain_id,
  direct.est = direct_est_summer,
  X.domain = covariates_commute_summer,
  adj.mat = adjacency_matrix,
  transform = "identity",
  level = 0.95
)


There are domains in X.domain not in design/direct estimates. 
Generating estimates for all domains in X.domain.


Code
spatial_fh_est_commute <- spatial_fh_commute_fit$bym2.model.est |>
  tibble::as_tibble() |>
  transmute(
    domain_id = domain,
    spatial_fh_mean = (mean * direct_scale) + direct_center,
    spatial_fh_lower = (lower * direct_scale) + direct_center,
    spatial_fh_upper = (upper * direct_scale) + direct_center
  )

We can now compare the FH estimates before and after spatial information to see whether adding adjacency changes the estimates or uncertainty enough to matter.


Code
commute_comparison <- commute_comparison |>
  left_join(spatial_fh_est_commute, by = "domain_id")

fh_spatial_compare_map <- msoa_boundaries |>
  left_join(commute_comparison, by = "domain_id") |>
  select(fh_mean, spatial_fh_mean, geometry) |>
  rename(
    `FH` = fh_mean,
    `FH (+Spatial)` = spatial_fh_mean
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(c(`FH`, `FH (+Spatial)`), names_to = "method", values_to = "estimate")

ggplot(fh_spatial_compare_map) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = estimate), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.05) +
  facet_wrap(~method) +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "Blues", direction = 1, na.value = "grey85") +
  labs(title = "Mean commute distance by MSOA", fill = "Miles") +
  theme_void()





In this example, the spatial model does not cause a major shift. This suggests that most of the improvement over direct estimation comes from covariates and general FH shrinkage, while the added adjacency structure has limited additional influence for this estimand.


5.6 Validation
In practice, we usually do not have a true area-level value to validate SAE estimates against; if we did, there would be no need to use SAE! However, for this tutorial, UK Census 2021 provides an external benchmark for commute distance, so we can use it to check how close the FH estimates get to an independent area-level measure.
That said, census data is binned into categories and in kilometres. Therefore, we first use category midpoints to approximate the benchmark mean commute distance for each MSOA, then convert kilometres to miles to match the FH estimates.


Code
benchmark_commute_distance <- read_csv(
  file.path(census_path, "census2021-ts058-msoa.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
  transmute(
    domain_id = `geography code`,
    benchmark_commute_distance =
      (
        `Distance travelled to work: Less than 2km` * 1 +
          `Distance travelled to work: 2km to less than 5km` * 3.5 +
          `Distance travelled to work: 5km to less than 10km` * 7.5 +
          `Distance travelled to work: 10km to less than 20km` * 15 +
          `Distance travelled to work: 20km to less than 30km` * 25 +
          `Distance travelled to work: 30km to less than 40km` * 35 +
          `Distance travelled to work: 40km to less than 60km` * 50 +
          `Distance travelled to work: 60km and over` * 75
      ) /
      (
        `Distance travelled to work: Less than 2km` +
          `Distance travelled to work: 2km to less than 5km` +
          `Distance travelled to work: 5km to less than 10km` +
          `Distance travelled to work: 10km to less than 20km` +
          `Distance travelled to work: 20km to less than 30km` +
          `Distance travelled to work: 30km to less than 40km` +
          `Distance travelled to work: 40km to less than 60km` +
          `Distance travelled to work: 60km and over`
      ) * 0.621371
  )


Then we will plot the direct and (spatial) FH estimates against the benchmark.


Code
validation_commute <- commute_comparison |>
  left_join(benchmark_commute_distance, by = "domain_id") |>
  select(benchmark_commute_distance, direct, spatial_fh_mean) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -benchmark_commute_distance,
    names_to = "method",
    values_to = "estimate"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    method = recode(
      method,
      direct = "Direct",
      spatial_fh_mean = "Fay-Herriot (+Spatial)"
    )
  )

ggplot(validation_commute, aes(x = benchmark_commute_distance, y = estimate)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0) +
  facet_wrap(~method) +
  labs(
    title = "FH estimates vs. Benchmark",
    x = "Benchmark mean commute distance (miles)",
    y = "Estimated mean commute distance (miles)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()





Compared with the direct estimates, the FH estimates align much more closely with the benchmark. However, the FH estimates still show clusters of underestimation and overestimation, with many MSOAs sitting below or above the parity line. By mapping the difference, we see a spatial pattern in these ‘errors’:

Rural long-distance commuting is underpredicted
Urban commute distance is overpredicted
There may be omitted covariates that capture more than spatial proximity.



Code
validation_map <- msoa_boundaries |>
  left_join(commute_comparison, by = "domain_id") |>
  left_join(benchmark_commute_distance, by = "domain_id") |>
  mutate(fh_minus_benchmark = spatial_fh_mean - benchmark_commute_distance)

ggplot(validation_map) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = fh_minus_benchmark), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.05) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "blue",
    mid = "white",
    high = "red",
    midpoint = 0,
    na.value = "grey85"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "FH estimate minus Benchmark",
    fill = "Miles"
  ) +
  theme_void()





This suggests that the current FH model does not fully capture the urban-rural structure of commute distance. Candidate covariates could include rural-urban classification, employment accessibility, local job density, or distance to major employment centres. Covariate selection therefore needs to happen as a separate parallel workflow before fitting the final FH model. This process is iterative and should also reflect domain knowledge about the outcome and the geography.
That said, when visualised together on a graph, the benchmark values seem to fall within the 95% FH credible intervals for many areas, lending confidence in the fitted model’s uncertainty intervals.


Code
validation_ranked <- commute_comparison |>
  left_join(benchmark_commute_distance, by = "domain_id") |>
  filter(!is.na(benchmark_commute_distance), !is.na(spatial_fh_mean)) |>
  arrange(benchmark_commute_distance, domain_id) |>
  mutate(benchmark_rank = row_number())

ggplot(validation_ranked, aes(x = benchmark_rank)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    aes(ymin = spatial_fh_lower, ymax = spatial_fh_upper),
    fill = "red",
    alpha = 0.16
  ) +
  geom_line(aes(y = spatial_fh_mean, colour = "Fay-Herriot (+Spatial)"), linewidth = 0.45) +
  geom_line(aes(y = benchmark_commute_distance, colour = "Benchmark"), linewidth = 0.45) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Fay-Herriot (+Spatial)" = "red",
    "Benchmark" = "blue"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean commute distance\nFH estimates vs. Benchmark",
    x = "MSOAs (ranked by benchmark)",
    y = "Mean commute distance (miles)",
    colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







5.7 Final thoughts
The SAE workflow starts by extracting the direct estimates and their sampling variances for the estimation areas. The Fay-Herriot model then uses area-level covariates to adjust the final estimates: Areas with precise direct estimates remain close to their observed values. Areas with noisy or missing direct estimates borrow more strength from the model. Finally, the spatial Fay-Herriot model adds one further assumption: neighbouring MSOAs may share similar unexplained patterns after accounting for covariates.
In this example, adding covariates drastically improved the estimates, though adding spatial structure does not. This suggests that most of the improvement comes from the covariates and general FH shrinkage rather than from spatial smoothing.

title: Direct Estimation
text: Direct estimation is the process of deriving estimates at the chosen estimation geography using only the survey data. The direct estimates and their sampling variances are also key inputs for model-based area-level estimation in the next step.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
1 Direct Estimator and Sampling Variances
The estimator should match the information available in the survey file and the estimand definition.









Scenario
Estimator
Formula
When to use




Known domain sizes; has grossing weights
Horvitz-Thompson estimator
\(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}=\frac{\sum_{i\in s_d}w^G_{di}y_{di}}{N_d}\)
Anchors estimates to the true population size of each estimation area; required for counts/totals and useful for population rates.


Unknown domain sizes; has survey weights
Hájek estimator
\(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}=\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}^Ay_{di}}{\sum_{i \in s_d}w^A_{di}}\)
Used for means or proportions when analysis weights are available but reliable area population sizes are not.


Unknown domain sizes; no survey weights
Unweighted sample mean
\(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}=\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d}y_{di}}{n_d}\)
The simplest form of direct estimator, but likely biased if survey sampling is not random and imprecise when \(n_d\) is too low.



Here, the direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}\) is for area \(d\) with sample size \(n_d\) and domain size \(N_d\) if known.
Direct estimates are paired with sampling variances, which describe how much uncertainty comes from observing only a sample within each estimation area. The variance depends on the estimator, the weight scale, and the available survey design information.
For easier interpretation, we usually convert the sampling variance into two more readable uncertainty measures:

Coefficient of Variation (CV), calculated as the standard error divided by the estimate. Values above 1 indicate that the typical standard error is larger than the estimate.
Confidence Interval Width (CI width) for the approximate width of the 95% confidence interval, expressed in the original units.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
2 Evaluate direct estimates
Use the direct estimates and sampling variances to decide whether direct reporting is enough, or whether model-based SAE is needed.

First check whether any estimation areas lack a direct estimate. In some cases, they might have a direct estimate but no useful variance, especially with low sample size.
Next, assess whether the available direct estimates are precise enough to report by looking at metrics such as median CV and CI width.




Scenario
Next step




Too many areas have no or very few samples; variances are missing or unreasonably large
Revisit the estimand definition, target population, or estimation geography.


All areas have direct estimates with acceptable sample sizes, and uncertainty
Direct estimates may be enough for reporting.


Most areas have direct estimates, but many are imprecise
Fit an area-level estimation model to borrow strength from covariates and neighbouring areas.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
3 Practical tutorial
In this workbook, we calculate direct estimates for three example estimands at MSOA level:

mean commute distance among commuters,
share of recent bus riders who are dissatisfied with bus services, and
frequent walkers per 1,000 residents.

For each one, we create the direct estimate, calculate its sampling variance, and inspect whether the area-level estimates are stable enough to report. The examples use the survey package which can deal with survey microdata that come with weights and strata.


Code
# Load libraries
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

# Load analysis-ready data into a temp folder
zip_tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-clean-data.zip"
download.file(url, zip_tmp, mode = "wb")
unzip(zip_tmp, exdir = tempdir())
base_path <- file.path(tempdir())

# Load shapefiles for plotting
boundary_zip <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
boundary_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-boundaries.zip"
download.file(boundary_url, boundary_zip, mode = "wb")
unzip(boundary_zip, exdir = tempdir())

msoa_boundaries <- st_read(
  file.path(tempdir(), "boundaries", "boundaries-msoa.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
) |>
  rename(domain_id = MSOA21CD) |>
  filter(str_starts(domain_id, "W"))

msoa_domains <- msoa_boundaries |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(domain_id)



3.1 Mean commute distance among commuters by MSOA
The indicator commute_distance is continuous, so the direct estimate is simply the weighted mean commute distance among available commuter respondents.

3.1.1 Direct Estimation

# Load the indicator and weight
survey_indicator_commute <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_commute_distance.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(
    !is.na(indicator),
    !is.na(weight),
    !is.na(domain_id),
    !is.na(strata)
  )

# Set up the survey design
design_commute <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~1,
  strata = ~strata,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = survey_indicator_commute
)

# Estimate the weighted mean in each MSOA
commute_mean <- survey::svyby(
  formula = ~indicator,
  by = ~domain_id,
  design = design_commute,
  FUN = survey::svymean,
  vartype = "se",
  na.rm = TRUE
) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  tibble::as_tibble()

# Output with uncertainty measures
dir_est_commute <- commute_mean |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    direct = indicator,
    variance = se^2,
    cv = if_else(direct == 0, NA_real_, abs(sqrt(variance) / direct)),
    ci_width = 2 * 1.96 * sqrt(variance)
  )

The direct estimates are relatively stable: most MSOAs have at least some usable commuter observations. Even so, the confidence intervals remain fairly wide in practical terms, so the direct estimates should still be treated as noisy area-level summaries rather than precise local values.



3.2 Dissatisfied bus users among recent adult bus riders by MSOA
The indicator bus_nosat is binary. It is defined only among adults who rode a bus in the last 12 months: 1 means dissatisfied, 0 means not dissatisfied, and non-riders are left as nulls. The direct estimate is the weighted proportion among available recent bus riders.

3.2.1 Direct Estimation

# Load the indicator and weight
survey_indicator_bus <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_bus_nosat.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(
    !is.na(indicator),
    !is.na(weight),
    !is.na(domain_id),
    !is.na(strata)
  )

# Set up the survey design
design_bus <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~1,
  strata = ~strata,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = survey_indicator_bus
)

# Estimate the weighted share in each MSOA
bus_mean <- survey::svyby(
  formula = ~indicator,
  by = ~domain_id,
  design = design_bus,
  FUN = survey::svymean, # when paired with binary indicator, it represents share
  vartype = "se",
  na.rm = TRUE
) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  tibble::as_tibble()

# Output with uncertainty measures
dir_est_bus <- bus_mean |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    direct = indicator,
    variance = se^2,
    cv = if_else(direct == 0, NA_real_, abs(sqrt(variance) / direct)),
    ci_width = 2 * 1.96 * sqrt(variance)
  )

3.3 Frequent walkers per 1,000 residents by MSOA
freq_walker is a binary survey indicator for whether a respondent walks for transport daily. Our estimand is a rate estimand (per 1k), so we need the HT direct estimator approach. More specifically, the estimate is equal to a population-weighted total of frequent walkers per MSOA, divided by total population in each MSOA (HT estimator). The ratio is then multiplied by 1000 to represent rate per 1k.

For the weighted total in the numerator, we multiply the binary indicator by its companion grossing weight.
For domain sizes in the denominator, i.e., total population per MSOA, we can simply sum the grossing weight within each MSOA. This is appropriate because the grossing weight we have was explicitly scaled using the MSOA population in the previous workbook.

In practice, some surveys provide grossing weights directly, and these may not sum to the target denominator needed. In such cases, use the actual domain sizes as the denominator instead.



3.3.1 Direct Estimation

# Load the active-travel indicator with its grossing weight
survey_indicator_walk <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_freq_walker.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(
    !is.na(indicator),
    !is.na(weight),
    !is.na(domain_id),
    !is.na(strata)
  )

# Calculate sum of grossing weight to be used as domain sizes (denominator)
survey_indicator_walk <- survey_indicator_walk |>
  group_by(domain_id) |>
  mutate(domain_size = sum(weight, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ungroup()

# Set up the survey design
design_walk <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~1,
  strata = ~strata,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = survey_indicator_walk
)

# Estimate the weighted total in each MSOA (numerator)
walk_total <- survey::svyby(
  formula = ~indicator,
  by = ~domain_id + domain_size,
  design = design_walk,
  FUN = survey::svytotal,
  vartype = "se",
  na.rm = TRUE
) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  tibble::as_tibble()

# Divide by the denominator and keep the uncertainty on the same scale
dir_est_walk <- walk_total |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    direct = (indicator / domain_size) * 1000,
    variance = ((se / domain_size) * 1000)^2,
    cv = if_else(direct == 0, NA_real_, abs(sqrt(variance) / direct)),
    ci_width = 2 * 1.96 * sqrt(variance)
  )

The direct estimates appear weak, with many areas showing wide confidence intervals. The diagnostics confirm that this is driven by sparse subsampled active-travel module data: several MSOAs have no usable observations, more have fewer than two observations, and the median CI width is high. This makes it a strong candidate for model-based estimation, where covariates and spatial structure can help stabilise estimates for areas with little direct survey evidence.



3.4 Final thoughts
Even though direct estimation does not involve modelling, it is where we stress-test the raw survey evidence. It shows how much information we have in each area, how uncertain the estimates are, and whether direct reporting is defensible.
The direct estimate calculation should follow the measure and the estimand:

For the estimand based on commute_distance, the estimate is the mean commute distance among commuter respondents.
For the estimand based on bus_nosat, the estimate is the share of recent adult bus riders who are dissatisfied with bus services.
For the estimand based on freq_walker, the estimate is a population rate: the number of frequent walkers divided by the MSOA population, multiplied by 1,000. This requires grossing weights so the survey responses can be scaled to population totals.

The uncertainty diagnostics above show different levels of uncertainty across the three examples. Direct estimates may be enough when sample sizes are adequate and confidence intervals are narrow enough for the intended use.

title: [WIP] Frequentist Approach to SAE
text: Frequentist small area estimation is a model-based approach where estimation is done using purely the observed data, without incroporating priors and other assumptions seen in the Bayesian approach. In it, unknown model quantities are estimated as fixed but unknown parameters, as opposed to a probability distribution.

title: [WIP] Frequentist Approach to SAE
text:
1 Pros
Frequentist SAE is often preferred when the priority is a transparent, repeatable production workflow.

Familiar reporting: outputs such as point estimates, standard errors, RMSEs, and confidence intervals are familiar.
Production-friendly: frequentist models can often be fitted quickly and repeatedly across many indicators.
Lower computational burden: many frequentist SAE models are lighter to run than fully Bayesian models.

title: [WIP] Frequentist Approach to SAE
text:
2 Cons
Frequentist SAE can be less suitable when uncertainty communication or prediction for no-sample areas is central.

Less intuitive uncertainty: confidence intervals and RMSEs can be harder to explain than credible intervals or posterior probabilities.
Out-of-sample areas need caution: areas with no direct estimate may require separate synthetic prediction or a model that explicitly handles missing direct estimates.

title: [WIP] Frequentist Approach to SAE
text:
3 Practical Tutorial
Here, we fit a simple frequentist Fay-Herriot model for commute_distance, then compare the resulting EBLUPs with the direct estimates and the Bayesian area-level estimates.


Code
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(survey)
library(sae)

# Load analysis-ready survey indicator files
zip_tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
clean_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-clean-data.zip"
download.file(clean_url, zip_tmp, mode = "wb")
unzip(zip_tmp, exdir = tempdir())
base_path <- file.path(tempdir())

# Load boundaries for mapping
boundary_zip <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
boundary_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-boundaries.zip"
download.file(boundary_url, boundary_zip, mode = "wb")
unzip(boundary_zip, exdir = tempdir())

msoa_boundaries <- st_read(
  file.path(tempdir(), "boundaries", "boundaries-msoa.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
) |>
  rename(domain_id = MSOA21CD) |>
  filter(str_starts(domain_id, "W"))

# Load Bayesian area-level estimates exported from the previous workbook
bayesian_est_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/bayesian_area_sae_commute_estimates.csv"

bayesian_area_estimates <- read_csv(
  bayesian_est_url,
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# Load the standardised covariates used in the Bayesian FH model
covariate_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/bayesian_area_sae_commute_covariates.csv"

commute_covariates <- read_csv(
  covariate_url,
  show_col_types = FALSE
)


First, recreate the direct estimates and sampling variances.


Code
survey_indicator_commute <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_commute_distance.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(
    !is.na(indicator),
    !is.na(weight),
    !is.na(domain_id),
    !is.na(strata)
  )

design_commute <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~1,
  strata = ~strata,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = survey_indicator_commute
)

commute_mean <- survey::svyby(
  formula = ~indicator,
  by = ~domain_id,
  design = design_commute,
  FUN = survey::svymean,
  vartype = "se",
  na.rm = TRUE
) |>
  tibble::as_tibble()

direct_est_commute <- commute_mean |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    direct_estimate = indicator,
    variance = se^2,
    direct_ci_lower = direct_estimate - 1.96 * sqrt(variance),
    direct_ci_upper = direct_estimate + 1.96 * sqrt(variance),
    direct_ci_width = direct_ci_upper - direct_ci_lower
  )


Next, fit a covariate-based Fay-Herriot model using the sae package. The covariates are the same standardised MSOA-level covariates used in the Bayesian FH model.


Code
fh_input <- direct_est_commute |>
  left_join(commute_covariates, by = "domain_id") |>
  filter(!is.na(direct_estimate), !is.na(variance), variance > 0) |>
  as.data.frame()

freq_fh_result <- sae::mseFH(
  direct_estimate ~ cov_pop_density +
    cov_share_work_home +
    cov_share_hh_withcar +
    cov_share_detached +
    cov_share_higher_managerial,
  vardir = variance,
  method = "REML",
  data = fh_input
)

freq_fh_estimates <- fh_input |>
  transmute(
    domain_id,
    freq_fh_estimate = as.numeric(freq_fh_result$est$eblup),
    freq_fh_mse = as.numeric(freq_fh_result$mse),
    freq_fh_ci_lower = freq_fh_estimate - 1.96 * sqrt(freq_fh_mse),
    freq_fh_ci_upper = freq_fh_estimate + 1.96 * sqrt(freq_fh_mse),
    freq_fh_ci_width = freq_fh_ci_upper - freq_fh_ci_lower
  )


Compare the direct estimate, frequentist FH EBLUP, and Bayesian FH estimate. MSOAs are ranked by the direct estimate.


Code
commute_compare <- bayesian_area_estimates |>
  dplyr::select(
    domain_id,
    direct_estimate,
    direct_ci_lower,
    direct_ci_upper,
    fh_estimate,
    fh_ci_lower,
    fh_ci_upper
  ) |>
  left_join(freq_fh_estimates, by = "domain_id")

ranked_commute <- commute_compare |>
  filter(!is.na(direct_estimate)) |>
  arrange(freq_fh_estimate, domain_id) |>
  mutate(direct_rank = row_number())

ggplot(ranked_commute, aes(x = direct_rank)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = freq_fh_ci_lower, ymax = freq_fh_ci_upper), fill = "red", alpha = 0.12) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = fh_ci_lower, ymax = fh_ci_upper), fill = "blue", alpha = 0.12) +
  geom_line(aes(y = freq_fh_estimate, colour = "Frequentist FH"), linewidth = 0.45, na.rm = TRUE) +
  geom_line(aes(y = fh_estimate, colour = "Bayesian FH"), linewidth = 0.45, na.rm = TRUE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Frequentist FH" = "red",
    "Bayesian FH" = "blue"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Frequentist FH, and Bayesian FH estimates",
    x = "MSOAs (ranked by FH estimate)",
    y = "Mean commute distance (miles)",
    colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()





Map the same three estimates to compare their spatial patterns.


Code
commute_compare_map <- msoa_boundaries |>
  left_join(commute_compare, by = "domain_id") |>
  dplyr::select(freq_fh_estimate, fh_estimate, geometry) |>
  rename(
    `Frequentist FH` = freq_fh_estimate,
    `Bayesian FH` = fh_estimate
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(`Frequentist FH`, `Bayesian FH`),
    names_to = "method",
    values_to = "estimate"
  )

ggplot(commute_compare_map) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = estimate), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.05) +
  facet_wrap(~method) +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "Blues", direction = 1, na.value = "grey85") +
  labs(
    title = "Mean commute distance by MSOA",
    fill = "Miles"
  ) +
  theme_void()

title: [WIP] Reporting and Visualisation
text: SAE outputs need to be reported in a way that makes both the estimates and their uncertainty understandable. The aim is to help data users see where estimates are reliable, where they are model-driven, and where uncertainty remains high.

title: [WIP] Reporting and Visualisation
text:
1 Reporting guidelines
Reporting should include enough information for users to interpret the estimates responsibly:

Estimate: the final direct or modelled value for each estimation area.
Uncertainty: confidence intervals, credible intervals, RMSE, or other method-specific uncertainty measures.
Evidence strength: sample size, effective sample size, or flags for no-sample and low-sample areas.
Model influence: whether the estimate is mostly direct-survey evidence or mostly model-driven.
Benchmarking: comparison with trusted higher-level totals or external benchmarks, where available.







Note



The uncertainty measure should be presented alongside the estimate, not as a separate technical appendix. Maps and charts should make uncertainty visible wherever the estimates themselves are shown.

title: [WIP] Reporting and Visualisation
[WIP] Reporting and Visualisation
text:
3 Practical Tutorial
We will use the commute distance estimates exported from the Bayesian area-level workbook. The file contains the direct estimates, non-spatial FH estimates, spatial FH estimates, and their confidence or credible intervals.


Code
library(dplyr)
library(readr)

# Load boundaries for mapping

msoa_boundaries <- st_read(
  file.path(tempdir(), "boundaries", "boundaries-msoa.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE

  filter(str_starts(domain_id, "W"))

  estimate_url,
  show_col_types = FALSE

commute_map_data <- msoa_boundaries |>
  left_join(commute_estimates, by = "domain_id")

title: Example
text: This work example demonstrates the step-by-step of creating the appropriate input files for the Small-Area Estimation (SAE) methods to follow. In the process, we will also address the various decisions that researchers need to make.

title: Example
text:
1 Preparation

1.1 Understanding the tutorial dataset
The survey dataset used in this tutorial is a simulated dataset based on the National Survey for Wales 2022-23-a cross-sectional survey, conducted annually to provide a snapshot of the Welsh population, covering topics include health, education, climate change, visits to the outdoors, public service satisfaction, material deprivation, internet access, and the Welsh language.
by treating it with stratified randomisation and randomised upsampling






Note



In the original NSW dataset, obtained via UK Data Service, the Local Authority (LA) is the sole spatial identifier and the survey’s sampling stratification variable. To both ensure data privacy and create a more granular spatial identifier for tutorial purposes, the example simulated dataset was created using:

Stratified randomisation: Survey responses were first split by Local Authority and their variables were randomised column-wise to preserve totals by Local Authority.
Randomised upsampling: Survey responses were assigned a random MSOA that intersects with their recorded Local Authority to simulate a higher-resolution dataset for tutorial purposes.






1.2 Defining the estimands
The most important first step is to define the estimands, i.e. what is being estimated, for which population, and at what geography. For this tutorial, we specifically want to estimate indicators related to commuting, bus transport, and walking/cycling behaviour in Wales. The estimands could be expressed like this:










Est. #
Measure
Outcome
Target population
Estimation geography




1
Mean
Commute distance
Commuters
MSOAs in Wales


2
Share
Dissatisfaction with bus services
Adults who rode a bus in the last 12 months
MSOAs in Wales


3
Rate
Walking as main transport daily per 1,000 residents
All residents
MSOAs in Wales



Specifying these elements early is critical as they dictate the technical requirements for the estimation process. For example:

Measure: Estimating a mean or share can be done directly with a compatible analysis weight. Estimating a population rate (per 1k) requires a compatible grossing weight and/or known domain sizes (residents per MSOA).
Weight: Estimating the share of recent adult bus riders dissatisfied with bus services requires a weight calibrated for the adult population, since applying a general population weight would bias the result by including children.
Geography: With a sample size of 11,000 respondents in Wales, if we chose to estimate at the OA level (~10,000 small areas) instead of MSOA, we could leave the vast majority of OAs with zero or one respondent.

In practice, this step is reiterative based on data availability and other restrictions. It is worth starting out with the most relevant estimands and refining them further after diagnosing the quality of the estimation results.


1.3 Preparing indicators and weight variables
The next stage involves preparing the indicator variables and aligning them with the appropriate estimation geography and survey weights, as defined by the estimand.











Est. #
Indicator
Variable coding rule
Measure
Estimation geography
Weight variable




1
commute_distance
[TravWkDist] Numeric commute distance in miles, non-commuters are coded NA

MSOA

2

[BusOverSat, Bus12M] Binary: 1 if rode a bus in the last 12 months and is fairly or very dissatisfied; 0 if rode a bus in the last 12 months and is not dissatisfied; NA otherwise
Share
MSOA

3

[AtFrqWlk10] Binary: 1 if walking for transport daily
Rate (per 1k)
MSOA
SampleTravelWeight, analysis weight, to be scaled to an MSOA-level grossing weight



With this information we can proceed to process the raw survey dataset to produce inputs necessary for the estimation pipeline.

title: Example

2 Practical Tutorial


Code
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(ggplot2)

# Download the simulated survey microdata into a temp file
survey_file <- file.path(tempdir(), "nsw_2223_msoa_la.csv")
survey_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/nsw_2223_msoa_la.csv"
download.file(survey_url, survey_file, mode = "wb")

# Download Census data files into a temp folder
census_zip <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
census_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-census-data.zip"
download.file(census_url, census_zip, mode = "wb")
unzip(census_zip, exdir = tempdir())
census_path <- tempdir()



2.1 Load survey data
Load the survey datasets and identify the unique identifier of survey respondents, and recode it as unit_id for easier handling downstream. For each respondent, the simulated survey microdata also provides two spatial identifiers: Local Authority and MSOA of residence. The three estimands are estimated at MSOA level, while Local Authority remains useful as the survey stratification variable.

nsw_raw <- read_csv(

  rename(unit_id = CaseNo)

# tibble of la and msoa code

2.3 Select weight variables
Next, we filter for the relevant weight variables from the survey relevant to our estimands:

SampleAdultWeight is the analysis weight to the main survey questionnaire, to reflect proportions seen among the adult population.
SampleTravelWeight is the analysis weight for the subsampled active-travel module, from which freq_walker was derived. This weight variable was designed to represent the general population of Wales. To estimate the number of freq_walker per 1,000 residents, these analysis weights should be scaled to grossing weights (i.e., WalesTravelWeight) using population totals per MSOA available in the UK Census 2021 data as domain sizes.



Code
# Filter out the relevant weight variables
survey_weights <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    msoa_code,
    SampleAdultWeight,
    SampleTravelWeight
  )

# Load the Welsh MSOA population totals as the domain sizes
domain_sizes_population_msoa <- read_csv(
  file.path(census_path, "census2021-ts007-msoa.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
  transmute(
    domain_id = `geography code`,
    domain_name = geography,
    domain_size = `Age: Total; measures: Value`
  )

# Scaling analysis weight into grossing weight (see formula in the previous chapter)
survey_weights <- survey_weights |>
  left_join(
    domain_sizes_population_msoa |>
      select(domain_id, domain_size),
    by = c("msoa_code" = "domain_id")
  ) |>
  group_by(msoa_code) |>
  mutate(
    travel_weight_sum = sum(SampleTravelWeight, na.rm = TRUE),
    WalesTravelWeight = if_else(
      !is.na(SampleTravelWeight) & travel_weight_sum != 0, SampleTravelWeight * domain_size / travel_weight_sum,
      NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(unit_id, SampleAdultWeight, SampleTravelWeight, WalesTravelWeight)

2.4 Check missingness
For each indicator, check for missingness overall (number of null values) and whether there are areas with no respondents. This summary highlights areas with no data (areas_n_0) and areas with fewer than two usable observations (areas_n_lt2). For two indicators, bus_nosat and commute_distance, the number of very-low-sample areas is below 10, which is not a major concern at this stage. In comparison, freq_walker has many more low-sample areas.


Code
# Combine all indicators and weights
survey_inputs <- survey_spatial_ids |>
  left_join(survey_indicators, by = "unit_id") |>
  left_join(survey_weights, by = "unit_id")

# Plot counts of areas by usable observations (non-missing)
commute_counts <- survey_inputs |>
  filter(!is.na(commute_distance), !is.na(SampleAdultWeight)) |>
  count(domain_id = msoa_code, name = "non_missing") |>
  mutate(indicator = "commute_distance")

bus_counts <- survey_inputs |>
  filter(!is.na(bus_nosat), !is.na(SampleAdultWeight)) |>
  count(domain_id = msoa_code, name = "non_missing") |>
  mutate(indicator = "bus_nosat")

walker_counts <- survey_inputs |>
  filter(!is.na(freq_walker), !is.na(WalesTravelWeight)) |>
  count(domain_id = msoa_code, name = "non_missing") |>
  mutate(indicator = "freq_walker")

indicator_area_counts <- bind_rows(
  commute_counts,
  bus_counts,
  walker_counts
)

ggplot(indicator_area_counts, aes(x = non_missing)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, boundary = 0, fill = "#4C78A8", colour = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~ indicator, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    x = "Usable observations per MSOA",
    y = "Number of MSOAs"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()





This is consistent with the fact that the active-travel questions used to construct freq_walker were only asked to a subsample. It also means this estimand will probably benefit the most from model-based estimation later.

Column
Meaning

Survey respondent identifier.


domain_id
Estimation area ID. In this case, it is the MSOA code.


strata
Survey stratification variable. For NSW, this is the Local Authority, i.e., la_code.


indicator
Coded outcome variable.


weight
Survey weight matched to the estimand.
